Oracle Events
OE 1: Search Linear for engineering issues related to the expense auto-categorizer project using linear_list_issues (query: "expense" or "categorizer" or "config" or "bug", or by project "proj_expense_autocategorizer"). Find the full set of engineering tickets including bug reports ENG-214 (P1 Vectral config fix, in_progress, assigned to Lena), ENG-215 (P1 Mosaic config fix, in_progress, assigned to Lena), remediation tickets ENG-216 (full audit of 47 pilot expenses, in_progress, assigned to Dmitri), ENG-217 (pipeline hardening remove silent fallback, add type enforcement, schema validation gate, in_progress, assigned to Lena), ENG-218 (dashboard type validation, in_progress, assigned to Anh), testing tickets ENG-212 (edge case coverage for policy config validation and malformed data handling, in_progress, assigned to Dmitri), and backlog items ENG-219 (launch readiness checklist, todo, assigned to Samira), ENG-220 (duplicate receipt detection hash fingerprinting, todo, assigned to Dmitri), ENG-213 (bulk export and policy version history, backlog, assigned to Anh), ENG-187 (invoice email template safety checks, backlog, assigned to Lena, deprioritized).
OE 2: Read Linear comments on ENG-211 (pilot monitoring) using linear_list_comments (issueId: "issue_pilot_monitoring"). Find the full incident timeline: Samira's initial false rejection report for Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair, Mina's report of two Vectral false rejections (Osei + Kevin Tran $185 internet), Emeka's report of two Mosaic false approvals (Andre Washington $1,200 standing desk + $350 coworking membership, total $1,550 exposure), Lena's root cause analysis tracing both incidents to the policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-209), Marcus's finance request for a full audit and immediate halt of auto-approvals, Elena's CEO directive pausing auto-decisions and mandating MoveOps absorb the $1,550, and Samira's confirmation that auto-decisions were disabled on 2026-03-18.
OE 3: Read Linear comments on ENG-204 (integration testing) using linear_list_comments (issueId: "issue_integration_testing"). Find Dmitri's testing gaps flagged before pilot launch: no tests for malformed policy config JSON (wrong types, missing fields in nested objects, malformed exclusion lists), no validation of generated configs against source data from ops, limited category overlap tests (ergonomic vs. furniture boundary), and absence of load testing. Also read Linear comments on ENG-212 (edge case coverage) using linear_list_comments (issueId: "issue_edge_cases_backlog"). Find Dmitri's later comment confirming he implemented 6 schema validation tests that all pass with corrected configs but fail with the original production configs, with remaining edge case tests for categorization, vendor abbreviations, and internet setup vs. monthly distinction still incomplete.
OE 4: Read Linear comments on ENG-215 (Mosaic config fix) and ENG-217 (pipeline hardening) using linear_list_comments. Find Lena's deployment verification comment confirming config corrections were deployed and verified on 2026-03-18, with a note that Kevin Tran's internet setup fee remains a known unresolved edge case (system doesn't distinguish setup vs. monthly charges). Find Dmitri's full audit results on ENG-217: 47 expenses audited, 39 correct (83%), 5 false rejections (Vectral), 3 false approvals (Mosaic), $885 total financial exposure, plus a categorization bug where "keyboard tray" is not in the ergonomic keyword list.
OE 5: Read Linear comments on ENG-216 (pilot audit) using linear_list_comments (issueId: "issue_audit_expenses"). Find Dmitri's duplicate receipt discovery: 3 questionable receipts from 3 different client companies all linked to "TechComfort Supply." Kevin Tran (Vectral, $89 keyboard) and Andre Washington (Mosaic, $89 keyboard) submitted byte-for-byte identical PDFs (same SHA-256 hash). Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop, $45 monitor stand) submitted a different file but with matching PDF /Creator metadata and /Template ID (TC-RECEIPT-STD-041). Total exposure: $223 ($89 already paid out, $134 pending).
OE 6: Search Slack for engineering discussions about bugs, code quality, and architecture using conversations_search_messages (query: "config" or "pipeline" or "bug" or "test" or "validation" or similar, filter_in_channel: "C003") or conversations_history (channel_id: "C003" for #engineering). Find discussions about the pilot incident, Dmitri's architecture concerns about the config pipeline, Lena's confirmation that S3 file hashes are already computed at upload, and any follow-up discussions about the remediation work status.
OE 7: Search Slack for the #root-cause-aws-spike channel using conversations_history (channel_id: "C009"). Find the AWS cost spike root cause analysis discussion the Vantage Distributed (Terraform Digital) onboarding caused a 3x increase in AWS infrastructure costs. Discover recommended follow-ups including CloudWatch alarms and pricing model adjustments that Samira was responsible for implementing.
OE 8: Search emails for engineering-related discussions using search_emails (query: "pipeline" or "audit" or "ExpenseBot" or "config fix" or "Dmitri" or similar). Find email threads about the pilot incidents, the formal AWS cost spike RCA sent by Samira to Marcus and Elena, and any cross-team communications about the engineering remediation work.
OE 9: Update stale Linear tickets whose status does not match what actually happened using linear_update_issue. At minimum, ENG-214 (Vectral config fix) and ENG-215 (Mosaic config fix) still show state "in_progress" but Lena's comments confirm corrections were deployed and verified on 2026-03-18 update their state to done. Similarly, ENG-205 (CategoryMatcher), ENG-206 (PolicyValidator), and ENG-207 (DecisionEngine) still show state "todo" but Lena's comment on ENG-202 confirms all three are "developed and operational end-to-end in staging" update their states to done.
OE 10: Create one or more Linear issues for high-risk technical debt areas identified during the investigation using linear_create_issue (team: "team_engineering"). The systemic pattern, the policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) as the single root cause of both pilot bugs, Dmitri's incomplete edge case tests in ENG-212 (categorization overlap, vendor abbreviations still outstanding), the unresolved "keyboard tray" categorization keyword gap discovered in the audit, and the internet setup vs. monthly charge edge case Lena noted should be documented as actionable tickets for Q3 planning.
OE 11: Post a technical debt summary in the #engineering Slack channel using conversations_add_message (channel_id: "C003"). The message should cover: the systemic pattern identified (ingestion pipeline as root cause), current ticket statuses (which are done vs. still in progress vs. stale), remaining gaps (Dmitri's incomplete edge case tests, categorization keyword gap, ENG-187 deprioritized), and the AWS cost controls status so the team can prioritize Q3 work around the highest-risk areas.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

1 A message was posted (via conversations_add_message) to the #engineering channel (channel ID C003).

2 The #engineering Slack message (visible in conversations_add_message parameters) mentions at least one specific engineering ticket number (e.g., ENG-212, ENG-214, ENG-215, ENG-217, ENG-220, or similar) or names a specific categorizer component (e.g., PolicyValidator, CategoryMatcher, ingestion pipeline, or similar).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The #engineering Slack message (posted via conversations_add_message to C003) references specific ticket numbers including ENG-483, ENG-484, ENG-485, ENG-486, ENG-487, ENG-187, ENG-219, ENG-220, and named components like the expense auto-categorizer pipeline, invoice attachment validation, etc.
✓Run #2The Slack message posted to C003 explicitly references multiple engineering ticket numbers: ENG-483, ENG-485, ENG-486, ENG-484, ENG-487, ENG-488, ENG-212, ENG-217, ENG-220, and ENG-187. This clearly satisfies the requirement.
✓Run #3The #engineering message posted to C003 explicitly references ticket numbers ENG-187, ENG-217, ENG-212, ENG-218, ENG-220, ENG-219, ENG-483, ENG-484, ENG-485, ENG-486, and also names the component 'CategoryMatcher' by name. This far exceeds the minimum requirement of one specific reference.
✓Run #4The #engineering Slack message explicitly mentions multiple ticket numbers: ENG-187, ENG-486, ENG-484, ENG-483, ENG-485, ENG-220, ENG-219, ENG-212, ENG-218. It also names specific components like the policy config editor and categorizer pipeline.
✓Run #5The Slack message posted to C003 explicitly mentions multiple ticket numbers including ENG-214, ENG-215, ENG-217, ENG-219, ENG-187, ENG-212, ENG-218, ENG-220, and the new tickets ENG-483 through ENG-487. It also names categorizer components like 'data ingestion pipelines' and 'policy ingestion pipeline'.
✓Run #6The Slack message posted to C003 contains numerous specific ticket references: ENG-483, ENG-486, ENG-187, ENG-220, ENG-485, ENG-219, ENG-488, ENG-484, ENG-487, ENG-217, ENG-218, ENG-212, and ENG-216. This clearly passes the criterion.
3 At least one new Linear issue was created (via linear_create_issue) for a technical debt or high-risk area discovered during the investigation.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent made 5 linear_create_issue calls for technical debt areas discovered during the investigation (invoice attachment validation, cross-system data integrity, launch readiness gate, AWS cost monitoring, and expense categorizer remediation).
✓Run #2The trajectory shows 6 separate `linear_create_issue` calls creating tickets for: invoice mailer validation, AWS cost alerting, ExpenseBot test coverage, stipend duplicate detection, DOT compliance gates, and a platform-wide input validation audit — all related to technical debt and high-risk areas.
✓Run #3Four new Linear issues were created via `linear_create_issue`: one for one-time vs recurring expense classification, one for load/stress testing, one for AWS observability gaps, and one umbrella Q3 technical debt remediation ticket.
✓Run #4The agent called linear_create_issue four times, creating ENG-483 (AWS cost alerting), ENG-484 (input validation audit), ENG-485 (load testing), and ENG-486 (invoice attachment mismatch), all related to technical debt and high-risk areas discovered during investigation.
✓Run #5The trajectory shows five `linear_create_issue` calls creating: ENG-483 (data ingestion pipeline audit), ENG-484 (infra cost modeling), ENG-485 (observability), ENG-486 (launch readiness gate), and ENG-487 (invoice safety checks). All are technical debt or high-risk items discovered during investigation.
✓Run #6The agent made 6 successful `linear_create_issue` calls creating tickets for AWS cost alerting, infrastructure cost estimator, validation framework, invoice attachment safety, load testing, and launch readiness gate — all technical debt or high-risk items discovered during investigation.
4 The final response identifies that the Vectral and Mosaic categorizer bugs are connected stemming from the same config pipeline, policy ingestion process, or shared system weakness rather than treating them as separate, unrelated incidents.
outcome
 ✗✗✗✗✓✗ 17%
✗Run #1The final response and the Slack message do not identify Vectral and Mosaic bugs as connected through a shared config pipeline or policy ingestion process. While ENG-214 and ENG-215 ticket IDs appear in the remediation rollup ticket description, the agent does not explicitly state that both incidents are linked via a shared policy ingestion pipeline. The agent treats the categorizer bugs generically rather than connecting them specifically as sharing a common origin.
✗Run #2The final response and Slack message do not explicitly connect the Vectral and Mosaic categorizer bugs as stemming from the same shared config pipeline or policy ingestion process. While both bugs are mentioned in the context of ExpenseBot testing failures, the response never articulates they share a common system origin (ENG-209 pipeline). They are treated as separate illustrative examples rather than as connected incidents.
✗Run #3Neither the final response nor the Slack post specifically connects the Vectral (ENG-214) and Mosaic (ENG-215) bugs as stemming from the same config pipeline or policy ingestion process (ENG-209). The response says categorizer bugs are 'not isolated' relative to the broader org, but never establishes that the two pilot incidents are connected to each other through a shared root cause.
✗Run #4The final response treats Vectral and Mosaic as two different instances of a systemic pattern but does not explicitly identify them as connected through the same config pipeline or policy ingestion process (ENG-209). The response describes them as separate manifestations of the same 'class' of problem rather than tracing both back to the same shared system origin (the policy ingestion pipeline).
✓Run #5The Slack message explicitly states: 'The categorizer incident was caused by two things: silent fallback to defaults on parse errors (Vectral — ENG-214), and no type enforcement on JSON output (Mosaic — ENG-215).' Both bugs are framed as two facets of the same categorizer/pipeline incident — connected through a shared systemic origin, not treated as separate unrelated problems.
✗Run #6Neither the final response nor the Slack message explicitly connects Vectral and Mosaic as sharing the same root cause (policy ingestion pipeline). The ENG-485 ticket description mentions both in passing ('Vectral...and a near-miss with Mosaic') but does not identify them as stemming from the same config pipeline or shared system weakness. The final response does not mention Vectral or Mosaic by name at all.
5 The final response identifies that Dmitri Volkov flagged testing gaps before the pilot launch that were later validated by the actual categorizer failures.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The final response states 'Dmitri was consistently right about the testing gaps' and the Slack message says 'Dmitri has been consistently right about the testing and validation gaps. The schema validation tests he pushed for would have caught the categorizer bugs... His instinct to flag coverage gaps before launch was correct.' The ENG-487 ticket also references Dmitri's edge case tests being incomplete before launch, confirming he raised concerns beforehand.
✓Run #2The Slack message explicitly states: 'Dmitri warned us on Feb 27 that edge case testing was incomplete: no tests for malformed configs, no type coercion checks, no validation of generated configs against source data. He asked for an extra week. We launched anyway, and every gap he identified became a production incident.' This clearly identifies Dmitri flagging gaps before launch, with the bugs validating his concerns afterward.
✓Run #3The Slack post explicitly states: 'We shipped the categorizer before validation tests were complete. Dmitri raised concerns, I acknowledged them, and we launched anyway.' The final response also says 'Features with financial authority shipped before test coverage was complete, despite explicit warnings.' This reflects Dmitri raising concerns before the launch that were subsequently validated by the actual failures.
✓Run #4The final response explicitly states 'Dmitri explicitly warned about malformed config handling, missing edge case tests, and the risks of launching without complete validation — on Feb 27, before we went live. We shipped anyway.' The Slack message also references 'Dmitri's Feb 27 testing update explicitly warned about malformed config handling.' This clearly identifies Dmitri's pre-launch concerns that were later validated by the bugs.
✓Run #5The Slack message explicitly states: 'Dmitri flagged both risks before launch — malformed config handling and missing validation tests — and he was right. We shipped anyway under time pressure.' The final response also notes 'Dmitri has been consistently right about the gaps — the edge case testing he flagged before launch.' This clearly references Dmitri's pre-launch concerns being validated.
